skill.name: pluto-pair
skill.description: Pair Codex with Pluto.jl notebooks using MCP tools. Use for discovering visible Pluto servers, attaching to running notebooks, opening notebooks headlessly, inspecting @bind controls, setting bond values, reading compact state, or exporting HTML.
skill.summary: Connect Claude Code to live Pluto.jl notebooks via MCP for interactive reactive computing
skill.author: EzraCerpac

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 14 static findings from SKILL.md. The 13 backtick execution patterns are documentation examples showing Ruby API calls for MCP tools, not actual command injection vulnerabilities. The weak cryptographic algorithm at line 3 appears in context of notebook server authentication, which is a local-only operation with no network exposure. Risk level set to medium due to external command presence, but all patterns are benign in context.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Documentation
high_findings.description: Line 3 references a weak cryptographic algorithm. Upon evaluation, this appears in the context of notebook server authentication documentation, not active code using insecure cryptography. The authentication is local-only between Codex and Pluto.jl servers on the same machine.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern detected but context suggests documentation rather than active vulnerable code. Local authentication does not expose this to external attackers.

medium_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Syntax in Documentation
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md contains 11 instances of Ruby backtick syntax. These are documentation examples showing MCP tool usage patterns, not executable code. The backticks reference MCP server commands for Pluto.jl integration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Backtick patterns in markdown documentation are standard code examples. No actual command injection vectors found - the skill uses MCP protocol for all interactions.

dangerous_patterns.title: MCP Protocol Usage for Notebook Control
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses the Model Context Protocol to interact with Pluto.jl notebooks. MCP is a structured communication protocol that does not allow arbitrary command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: MCP tools provide controlled interfaces for specific operations: server discovery, notebook attachment, cell inspection, and HTML export. No arbitrary shell access.

content.value_statement: Scientific computing workflows require interactive notebooks, but switching between Codex and Pluto.jl breaks concentration. This skill bridges Claude Code with live Pluto.jl servers so you can query notebook state, inspect reactive bindings, and export results without leaving your coding flow.
actual_capabilities: Discover Pluto.jl servers running on the local network
Attach to running notebooks and read their current state
Inspect @bind controls and read their current values
Set bond values to update reactive variables programmatically
Open notebooks headlessly for batch operations
Export notebooks to standalone HTML files
limitations: Requires Pluto.jl and the PlutoPair MCP server to be installed locally
Cannot modify notebook source code directly, only read and set bond values
Network discovery may fail if firewalls block UDP broadcast on the local network

use_cases.title: Debug Reactive Julia Notebooks
use_cases.description: When a Pluto.jl notebook produces unexpected results, use this skill to inspect all @bind controls, see their current values, and trace how reactive dependencies propagate through the notebook.
use_cases.target_user: Julia data scientists debugging interactive notebooks

use_cases.title: Automate Parameter Sweeps
use_cases.description: Run parameter sweeps by setting bond values programmatically. Codex can loop through different configurations, reading results after each run to determine next steps.

use_cases.title: Export Notebooks for Sharing
use_cases.description: After refining a notebook through interactive development, export it to standalone HTML so colleagues can view it without installing Julia or Pluto.jl.
use_cases.target_user: Educators and researchers sharing reproducible analyses

prompt_templates.title: Find Running Notebooks
prompt_templates.prompt: What Pluto.jl notebooks are running on this machine?

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Reactive State
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me all the @bind controls and their current values in the notebook at port 1234.

prompt_templates.title: Set Parameters
prompt_templates.prompt: Set the learning_rate bond to 0.001 and the batch_size bond to 64 in the notebook on port 1234.

prompt_templates.title: Export for Sharing
prompt_templates.prompt: Export the notebook from port 1234 to a standalone HTML file at /tmp/analysis.html.

output_examples.output:
Found 2 Pluto.jl servers:
- Port 1234: 'Neural Network Training' (running for 2 hours)
- Port 5678: 'Data Visualization' (idle for 15 minutes)

output_examples.input: Show bonds in notebook on port 1234
output_examples.output:
Active @bind controls:
- learning_rate: 0.001
- batch_size: 64
- epochs: 100
- optimizer: "Adam"

Successfully exported to /tmp/neural-net.html (1.2 MB)
Open this file in any browser to view the interactive notebook.

best_practices: Start your Pluto.jl notebooks before invoking Codex commands that need to attach to them
Use descriptive notebook titles since they appear in the server list for easier identification
Close notebooks when done to free system resources, especially on memory-constrained machines
anti_patterns: Do not try to set bond values that conflict with notebook validation logic; the notebook will revert changes
Avoid attaching to notebooks while they are executing long-running computations; wait for idle state
Do not use this skill to modify notebook source code directly; use it only for reading state and setting bonds

faq.question: What is Pluto.jl?
faq.answer: Pluto.jl is a reactive notebook environment for Julia. Unlike traditional notebooks, Pluto cells automatically re-run when their dependencies change, making it excellent for exploratory computation and teaching.

faq.question: How does this skill connect to notebooks?
faq.answer: The skill uses Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools to communicate with a local PlutoPair server. You must install and run the PlutoPair MCP server for this skill to function.

faq.question: Can I modify notebook source code with this skill?
faq.answer: No. This skill can only read notebook state and set @bind bond values. To modify code, you must edit the notebook file directly using a text editor or the Pluto.jl interface.

faq.question: Why are some notebooks not showing up in discovery?
faq.answer: Notebooks must be started with Pluto.run() to become discoverable. Notebooks opened in a browser without starting the server programmatically will not appear in the server list.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use with sensitive data?
faq.answer: Yes. All communication happens locally between Codex and Pluto.jl on the same machine. No data is transmitted over the network except for local network discovery of other Pluto servers.

faq.question: What happens if I set an invalid bond value?
faq.answer: Pluto.jl validates all bond assignments. If you set an invalid value, the notebook will reject it and revert to the previous value. Check the validation rules in the notebook source code.
